channel.title: NYT > U.S. News
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Bad News for Friggatriskaidekaphobics: 2026 Has Three Fridays the 13th
item.description: It’s the first time since 2015 that the combination of the day and date associated with bad luck has recurred three times in a calendar year.
item.dc:creator: Sopan Deb
item.media:credit: Underwood Archives/Getty Images
item.media:description: The Gregorian calendar runs in 400-year cycles, offering 59 years in which the 13th falls on a Friday three times. 2026 is one of them.

item.title: Suspect in Michigan Synagogue Attack Worked at Popular Restaurant
item.description: The authorities identified the suspect as a naturalized U.S. citizen. Residents of Dearborn Heights, Mich., said he took orders at the business.
item.dc:creator: Julie Bosman, Ryan Patrick Hooper and Sheelagh McNeill
item.media:credit: Sarah Rice for The New York Times
item.media:description: A heavy police presence in West Bloomfield Township, Mich., after the attack.

item.title: Trump Removes Sanctions on Russia to Help Oil Flow Amid Iran Conflict
item.description: Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said it was “unfortunate” that the move could benefit Russia, but maintained that it was only for the short term.
item.dc:creator: Alan Rappeport
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said on Thursday that Russia would not benefit significantly from the sanctions relief but acknowledged that it would see some financial benefit.

item.title: What We Know About Michigan Synagogue Attack on Temple Israel
item.description: The attacker who drove into Temple Israel in West Bloomfield Township, a 41-year-old naturalized U.S. citizen, is dead. No one else was killed.
item.dc:creator: Alexandra E. Petri
item.media:credit: Sarah Rice for The New York Times
item.media:description: Emergency personnel after an attack at Temple Israel in West Bloomfield Township, Mich., on Thursday.

item.title: A Weakened Iran Hits Back by Strangling the Vital Strait of Hormuz
item.description: The threats to shipping in the Strait of Hormuz are complicating President Trump’s calculations about how and when to end the war.
item.dc:creator: Anton Troianovski, Peter Eavis, Julian E. Barnes and Greg Jaffe
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Benoit Tessier/Reuters
item.media:description: An oil tanker anchored in Muscat, Oman, on Thursday. Iran has throttled traffic through the Strait of Hormuz, through which a fifth of the world’s oil was passing before the war began.

item.title: Treasure Hunter Released From Prison After Refusing to Turn Over Gold Coins
item.description: Thomas G. Thompson was held in contempt for 10 years for repeatedly denying he knew the whereabouts of 500 missing gold coins.
item.dc:creator: Remy Tumin
item.media:credit: Imagn
item.media:description: Thomas G. Thompson in court in 2018. He discovered the S.S. Central America, a steamship that sank in 1857 with tons of coins, bars and nuggets of California gold worth millions of dollars.

item.title: Florida Republicans Pass Bill Requiring Proof of Citizenship to Vote
item.description: The proposed law, which would not take effect before this year’s midterm elections, was modeled in part on President Trump’s top legislative priority in Congress.
item.dc:creator: Patricia Mazzei and Nick Corasaniti
item.media:credit: Scott McIntyre for The New York Times
item.media:description: The law’s passage would make Florida the most populous state in the country with proof-of-citizenship requirements for voters.

item.title: With Disputed Legal Maneuver, Trump Tries to Set Policy Without Legislation
item.description: By suing Republican states and making sharp reversals in old cases, the Trump administration is using courts to fast-track major shifts in policy.
item.dc:creator: Zach Montague
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: In some cases, the legal maneuver has helped the Trump administration lock in changes at the federal level that could persist for years after President Trump leaves office.

item.title: Places of Worship, Magnets for Violence: Synagogue Attacks Have Risen
item.description: The attack on Temple Israel is just the most recent in a string of attacks. They have taken place in the United States and around the world.
item.dc:creator: Pooja Salhotra
item.media:credit: Sarah Rice for The New York Times
item.media:description: Antisemitic incidents in the United States have occurred with increasing frequency over the past decade, according to the Anti-Defamation League.

item.title: Executive and Judicial Branches Spar Over Control of Federal Courthouses
item.description: The head of the General Services Administration said a proposal to transfer control of courthouse buildings to the judiciary was a bad idea.
item.dc:creator: Mattathias Schwartz
item.media:credit: Allison Robbert/Associated Press
item.media:description: Edward C. Forst, the head of the General Services Administration, in January.

item.title: Temple Israel Was Founded in 1941, Dedicated to the Formation of a Jewish State
item.description: The temple is one of the largest Reform institutions in America, with thousands of families and an early education center.
item.dc:creator: Katherine Rosman
item.media:credit: Sarah Rice for The New York Times
item.media:description: Police swarmed to the suburban house of worship after the attack.

item.title: Trump Targets Forced Labor in Global Tariff Scheme
item.description: The Trump administration began a trade investigation Thursday into whether dozens of countries have policies to combat forced labor.
item.dc:creator: Ana Swanson
item.category: Office of the United States Trade Representative
item.media:credit: Mark Abramson for The New York Times
item.media:description: Trucks leaving the Port of Los Angeles last May. The latest trade investigation is part of an effort by the Trump administration to resurrect a global system of tariffs struck down by the Supreme Court last month.

item.title: Attack at Detroit-Area Synagogue Echoes Incident at a Michigan Church Last Year
item.description: For the second time in about six months, a driver rammed a vehicle into a house of worship in Michigan. Four people died as a result of last September’s attack in Grand Blanc Township.
item.dc:creator: Christina Morales
item.media:credit: Rubini Naidu for The New York Times
item.media:description: Community members waiting near a secured area around the Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints meetinghouse in Grand Blanc Township, Mich.

item.title: Attack on Synagogue Comes Amid Significant Rise in Antisemitic Incidents
item.description: The number of assaults and threats against Jews has increased in recent years, with anxiety growing further since the start of the U.S.-Israeli war with Iran.
item.dc:creator: Ruth Graham and Campbell Robertson
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Imani Khayyam for The New York Times
item.media:description: Damage to the Beth Israel synagogue in Jackson, Miss., in January.

item.title: $42 Million Verdict for Iraqi Victims of U.S. Abuse Is Upheld on Appeal
item.description: The damages would be paid by a Virginia contractor that supplied interrogators to the U.S. military after the 2003 invasion of Iraq.
item.dc:creator: Mattathias Schwartz
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Shuran Huang for The New York Times
item.media:description: Salah Hasan Al-Ejaili, a former detainee at the notorious Abu Ghraib prison in Iraq, in 2024.

item.title: Will Illinois’ Democratic Primary for Senate Divide Black Voters?
item.description: Two Black female candidates may split Democratic primary voters in Illinois, and anger is growing at well-funded efforts to widen the divide.
item.dc:creator: Reid J. Epstein, Lisa Lerer and Shane Goldmacher
item.media:credit: Photographs by Joshua Lott for The New York Times
item.media:description: Lt. Gov. Juliana Stratton, left, and Representative Robin Kelly are at the center of a fight for Black voters in the Democratic primary race for Senate in Illinois.

item.title: Senate Again Deadlocks on Homeland Security Funding as Shutdown Persists
item.description: The fight over restrictions on immigration agents has prolonged the funding lapse for T.S.A., Coast Guard and more into a second month, as airports experience screening delays.
item.dc:creator: Carl Hulse
item.media:credit: Nam Y. Huh/Associated Press
item.media:description: A passenger at O’Hare International Airport in Chicago last month.

item.title: F.B.I. Joins Search for Air Force General Missing for 2 Weeks
item.description: Before he retired, Maj. Gen. William N. McCasland oversaw a research laboratory at Wright-Patterson Air Force Base near Dayton, Ohio.
item.dc:creator: Michael Levenson
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: United States Air Force
item.media:description: Maj. Gen. William N. McCasland, 68, left his home near Albuquerque on Feb. 27, and has not been seen since.

item.title: How Trump’s Election Lie Could Affect 2026 Midterms
item.description: The Trump administration is putting the weight of the federal government behind his false claims about the 2020 election in order to investigate key swing states ahead of the midterms. Nick Corasaniti, a New York Times reporter focusing on elections, homes in on the states that have become the prime targets and why.
item.dc:creator: Nick Corasaniti, Coleman Lowndes, James Surdam, Nikolay Nikolov and Rafaela Balster

item.title: Police Respond to Shooting at Temple Israel in Michigan
item.description: It was not immediately clear if anyone had been injured, though smoke could be seen pouring from the building.
item.dc:creator: Mitch Smith, Julie Bosman, Neil Vigdor and Orlando Mayorquín

item.title: People in This Mississippi City Faced Off With Elon Musk. They Lost.
item.description: Despite locals’ concerns about noise and possible pollution, Mississippi is allowing Mr. Musk’s company to operate gas turbines there that will help power its chatbot.
item.dc:creator: Emily Cochrane
item.media:credit: Brad J. Vest for The New York Times
item.media:description: The xAI power plant in Southaven, Miss.

item.title: Flowers Are Blooming in California’s Death Valley
item.description: Visitors are flocking to see a bonanza of wildflowers that has transformed this barren desert.
item.dc:creator: Amy Graff and Gabriela Bhaskar

item.title: Senate Resoundingly Passes Housing Bill, but Challenges Lie Ahead
item.description: The lopsided vote to approve the measure was a rare bit of election-year bipartisanship on a major affordability issue, but G.O.P. disputes and President Trump’s disinterest have left its fate uncertain.
item.dc:creator: Ronda Kaysen
item.media:credit: Will Crooks for The New York Times
item.media:description: Home construction in South Carolina earlier this year.

item.title: Old Dominion University Shooting Being Investigated as Act of Terrorism, Officials Say
item.description: The F.B.I. said the shooting, which wounded two other people at the university, in Norfolk, Va., was being investigated as an act of terrorism.
item.dc:creator: Campbell Robertson, Devlin Barrett and Christine Hauser
item.media:credit: Kendall Warner/The Virginian-Pilot, via Associated Press
item.media:description: The police responded to a report of a shooting on the campus of Old Dominion University in Norfolk, Va., on Thursday morning.

item.title: Ron DeSantis Wants Speedy Executions, and Lots of Them
item.description: After President Trump urged states to recommit themselves to capital punishment, Florida started to put prisoners to death at rates not seen in the state’s modern history.
item.dc:creator: Adam Liptak
item.media:credit: Curt Anderson/Associated Press
item.media:description: The Florida State Prison in Starke, Fla.

item.title: Trump Endorses Jake Paul, Who Isn't Running for Office
item.description: The president called Mr. Paul, a boxer and provocative influencer, a “great guy” and predicted that he would run for an elected position.
item.dc:creator: Tim Balk
item.media:credit: Doug Mills/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump predicted that Jake Paul, who rose to prominence as a vlogger and provocateur, would run for office “in the not-too-distant future.”

item.title: James Clyburn to Run for House Again, Defying Push for Generational Change
item.description: The Democratic power broker had kept his plans under wraps but signaled he wanted to be around to see the first Black speaker elected, a milestone the party is well positioned to reach next year.
item.dc:creator: Annie Karni
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: Representative James Clyburn, Democrat of South Carolina, was first elected to Congress in 1992.

item.title: A Statue of Trump and Epstein Re-enacting ‘Titanic’ Pose Appears on National Mall
item.description: The installation is the latest in a series of satirical statues created by an anonymous group of artists called the Secret Handshake.
item.dc:creator: Ashley Ahn
item.media:credit: Evelyn Hockstein/Reuters
item.media:description: A statue titled “The King of the World” depicting President Trump and Jeffrey Epstein.

item.title: San Francisco’s Chinatown Celebrated Eileen Gu. Others Are More Conflicted.
item.description: Conservatives have rebuked Ms. Gu, who was born in the United States but won Olympic medals for China. The reaction has sparked conversations among Chinese Americans about identity and straddling two worlds.
item.dc:creator: Amy Qin and Coral Murphy Marcos
item.media:credit: Mike Kai Chen for The New York Times
item.media:description: Ms. Gu has often said that “When I’m in the U.S., I’m American, but when I’m in China, I’m Chinese.”

item.title: Trump Administration Suggests Tariff Refunds May Take Significant Time
item.description: The government must update a federal court on Thursday about its timeline for returning roughly $166 billion in illegal duties.
item.dc:creator: Tony Romm
item.media:credit: Ruth Fremson/The New York Times
item.media:description: To refund any tariffs collected during the most punishing phase of President Trump’s trade war, the government said it would need more time.

item.title: How Hegseth Came to See Moral Purpose in War as Weakness
item.description: Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth’s bellicose and vengeful rhetoric describing the military’s war in Iran grew out of his experience in Iraq.
item.dc:creator: Greg Jaffe
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Doug Mills/The New York Times
item.media:description: Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth attending the dignified transfer of six troops killed in the Middle East last week. For the mission in Iran, Mr. Hegseth signed off on the name “Epic Fury,” which connotes retribution and rage.

item.title: Videos of ICE Shooting in Texas Capture a Confused and Fatal Encounter
item.description: Officials said a 23-year-old Texan had intentionally run over an officer, a claim his family and friend denied. Newly released footage leaves the truth murky.
item.dc:creator: Pooja Salhotra, Devon Lum, Alexander Cardia, Dmitriy Khavin and Edgar Sandoval

item.title: How Trump Officials Embraced an Animal Rights Campaign
item.description: The Trump administration is curbing animal experiments in response to shifts in public opinion, technological advances, years of animal rights advocacy and the work of a conservative activist.
item.dc:creator: Sheryl Gay Stolberg
item.category: People for the Ethical Treatment of Animals
item.media:credit: Hannah Yoon for The New York Times
